# Self-Driving Cars: A Technology Not Yet Ready for the Road

Of the numerous advantages to self-driving cars, the most notable include the elimination of human error and distracted driving, which together make up a majority of car accidents; redistributing the time people spend driving to other tasks, such as reading, chatting, and eating; and providing a safe and independent mode of transportation for people with disabilities, elderly adults who can no longer drive, and intoxicated individuals. On the other hand, the price, security risks, and potential flaws and incapabilities in cases of failure of traffic signals, inclement weather, and inaccuracy of GPS mapping, upon which self-driving cars rely, all provide compelling evidence against the case for self-driving cars.

A large consideration, especially with new advances in technology, is the safety of our personal information as consumers. Self-driving cars would be of utmost interest to hackers and large corporations due to their collection of personal data. Further, because of the $100,000 price point, the benefits of self-driving cars, specifically the claim that car accidents due to human error will decrease, can never be attained. In order for self-driving cars to thrive, they must be the majority on the road, and based on the current socioeconomic climate in the United States, this will never be the case.

While one could argue that in situations such as hazardous driving conditions or failure of automated traffic signals, a quick and simple way of switching to a manual driver mode would eliminate these problems, drivers may not respond quickly enough to the situation, particularly if they are using their newfound free time to eat, talk, or read, or if they have an impairment due to age, ability-level, or intoxication that would prohibit their ability to drive in the first place. In the case of GPS inaccuracy, which many users can attest to, a driver may not realize that the car is taking them in the wrong direction. Moreover, the situation can increase in danger if the car tries to turn down a one-way street or avoid a road that does not appear to exist in the data.

Overall, the risks associated with self-driving cars outweigh the benefits. Despite the luxury of enjoying more free time and increased accessibility to owning a car, self-driving cars are not a reliable mode of transportation. They are vulnerable to hackers, machine-error due to the lack of reliability of GPS mapping, and are not projected to decrease accidents until their use is widespread—which, due to their exorbitant pricing, is not likely to happen in the near or distant future. Until manufacturers can address these critical safety and security concerns while reducing costs to make the technology accessible to the average consumer, self-driving cars remain an impractical solution to modern transportation challenges.

# Self-Driving Cars: Promise and Practicality

Self-driving cars have become the craze of the new world. Not only would they be fuel efficient and reduce the number of accidents on the road, they would also allow drivers the opportunity to interact with their friends in the car instead of focusing on the road. Transportation by car would instead be similar to a train, where you can leisurely admire the view and chat with friends while you cruise towards your destination. However, self-driving cars are not infallible. Leaving our lives to the decisions of a machine is no simple task. Should the technology fail, will drivers who lack practice be able to handle the situation? Being suddenly thrown into such a scenario would cause panic and make drivers more prone to causing accidents.

Despite these concerns, self-driving cars represent the way of the future. They would greatly reduce the number of accidents on the road. Since a large percentage of road accidents are attributed to human error, employing autonomous vehicles would eliminate this key cause of accidents that occurs today. By removing the human element from driving, there will be fewer chances of misjudgments, because machines execute their programming consistently. Self-driving vehicles also help prevent traffic jams. Many traffic jams occur because cars tailgate each other so closely that they must constantly accelerate and brake, creating congestion. Machines would determine the optimum speed and distance to maintain smooth traffic flow. Combined with the safe distances self-driving cars would maintain, this technology would not only reduce accidents but also improve overall traffic conditions.

Yet our current technology falls short of what is necessary. GPS systems sometimes direct drivers down dead ends, and at intersecting roads, they struggle to identify which road the vehicle is on, providing conflicting instructions. Additionally, GPS does not function underground or in tunnels, limiting available routes for self-driving cars. Current technology is simply not advanced enough for self-driving cars to be truly independent. This means inexperienced drivers could be thrust into control at any moment, greatly increasing the risk of accidents. Furthermore, the technology required to build accurate self-driving cars would be prohibitively expensive. These vehicles would become luxury items affordable only to the wealthy, limiting the number on the road. This limitation is problematic because self-driving cars rely on information shared by other autonomous vehicles—meaning the more self-driving cars in operation, the better they perform. With such a high barrier to entry and no current advantage, self-driving cars are not yet a viable option.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars represent a promising step forward, they remain more fantasy than reality. Unless technology improves significantly in both affordability and accuracy, widespread adoption of autonomous vehicles will remain a distant dream rather than an immediate solution to our transportation challenges.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Technology Worth Pursuing

The advantages and disadvantages of self-driving cars have become a hot topic of discussion in mainstream media outlets since the public sphere became aware of the role these futuristic vehicles could play in the not-too-distant future. First, there are many perceived disadvantages stated by those who doubt the potential benefits this advanced machinery could have on modern life. These potential issues include the sheer cost of the software, the potential risk associated with technological failures outside of the car, as well as the security risks of the software itself. The cost of the software will likely be the primary obstacle for car companies attempting to flood the market with self-driving cars. However, as some states have done with electric vehicles, there could be an opportunity for federal tax credits. For instance, in California, people purchasing new electric vehicles can receive a tax credit up to a cap of $7,500 depending on the size of the car's battery, and we could see a similar incentive allotted to self-driving cars in the future as their software has the potential to greatly decrease car insurance costs. Other skeptics have shed light on the possibility of self-driving cars failing in circumstances where traffic signals have failed and police move to the intersection to direct traffic. This issue could be overcome by providing a manual override switch that would allow a driver to take control of the wheel for a period of time so the driver may avoid any obstacles while keeping the basic safety software active in the event of a potential accident. The potential for the software to be targeted by hackers is another real issue that could become a major problem if the software isn't adequately protected. However, as long as information identifying the owner of each vehicle is kept separate from the car's computer software and the software that controls the cars is linked to a kill switch that could safely pull the car off the road and alert the driver, any substantial damage associated with this threat could be minimized even though the potential to completely protect from it is limited.

There are, however, many advantages that I believe outweigh any potential negatives associated with this revolutionary technology. These advantages include a potential dramatic reduction in crashes caused by distracted or impaired drivers, a potential decrease in car insurance costs which could be financially beneficial to owners in the long run, and the potential for this software to remove the obstacle of rush hour traffic while providing people with lower incomes and/or limited mobility with an option to move around a city. Around 80% of accidents on the road are caused by human error, and a majority of the accidents caused by human error can be linked to driving while distracted—either by a phone, conversation within the car, or events happening outside—or driving while under the influence of alcohol or drugs. This technology would greatly decrease the number of accidents due to these factors as people under the influence of these factors would have no direct role in the control of the vehicle.

Paired with this decrease in crashes is the potential for a decrease in car insurance premiums and fees for all drivers. As roads become safer and accidents become rarer, there would be less reason for people to purchase comprehensive car insurance. Furthermore, manufacturers who are making vast amounts of money from car sales could cover the cost of the insurance, which would provide consumers with more money to use in the market and stimulate economic growth.

Additionally, the potential of platooning and communication between all self-driving cars in an area could dramatically reduce traffic buildups on off-ramps, which substantially increase travel time for all commuters. This decrease in travel time, paired with the time professionals would have during their chauffeured commute, could allow them to spend more time on aspects of life arguably more important than work—such as family life, good mental and physical health, and personal projects. This increased quality of life could overall increase happiness and productivity both at work and at home.

Furthermore, the role of self-driving cars to chauffeur their owners from point A to point B could be particularly beneficial for the percentage of the population with limited mobility. These vehicles would allow elderly citizens and people with disabilities to explore their city and perform daily tasks that they otherwise would not be able to accomplish due to their inability to operate a vehicle independently. This expanded mobility could significantly improve their quality of life and independence.

In conclusion, the benefits of self-driving cars outweigh the potential negatives, provided this technology is developed with a long-term vision rather than as a short-term solution. As we continue to advance this technology and address legitimate safety and security concerns, self-driving cars have the potential to revolutionize transportation, reduce accidents, improve traffic flow, and enhance the quality of life for millions of people. With proper regulation, investment, and oversight, self-driving cars represent a promising future for modern transportation.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Technology Worth Pursuing

What do Google, Tesla and Uber seem to be drawn to over recent years? Self-driving cars. The technology seems premature for everyday life, especially after several high-profile crashes led Uber to pull out of self-driving services. Self-driving cars appear to struggle in circumstances that significantly deviate from programmed conditions, such as areas lacking GPS information or extreme weather that prevents road visualization. However, it is important to recognize that these are merely limitations, not fatal flaws. These challenges should not cause us to abandon the significant progress already made in this technology.

Despite current setbacks, self-driving cars promise substantial advantages. While glitches in self-driving systems are undeniably dangerous, traditional automobiles face similar risks. Several renowned companies—GM, Volkswagen, and Audi—have issued major recalls for brake problems that endangered countless drivers. With self-driving cars, problems can often be addressed through software updates without requiring costly recalls of entire fleets. This approach saves manufacturers money and resources while sparing drivers the time and inconvenience of recalls. Beyond cost savings, self-driving cars can save lives. Countless accidents result from driver distractions ranging from texting to social media. By eliminating these distractions, self-driving cars can provide safer conditions for both drivers and pedestrians. Additionally, like all technological advances, autonomous vehicles will reclaim time spent on mundane activities, allowing individuals to boost productivity. Furthermore, self-driving cars can reduce common traffic inefficiencies caused by human driving variability, alleviating congestion and parking shortages during rush hours.

As self-driving technology matures and develops, it will greatly enhance everyday life for both drivers and non-drivers alike, relieving the burdens of heavy traffic and reducing accidents. Most importantly, it will save lives. While some argue that this technology will make driver's education obsolete, if it makes drunk driving education and its cautionary videos obsolete, the transition may well be worth it.

However, computers do make mistakes, and when they do, the consequences can be severe. The most obvious problem with self-driving cars is also a safety issue. What if the car's system crashes in the middle of the road? Can the driver immediately take control? When addressing safety, numerous problems naturally arise, and self-driving car systems are not yet ready to answer all these concerns or convince customers that they can solve them. Furthermore, if accidents do occur, who bears responsibility? This raises both moral and legal questions. Additionally, the introduction of self-driving cars will disrupt several markets, which will likely generate significant opposition.

Personally, I am against the widespread deployment of self-driving cars to the general public. However, I support continued research and small-scale testing, perhaps at science conferences, to explore what technology can achieve. My primary concern is safety. We know that computer systems sometimes crash unexpectedly. While such crashes are merely inconvenient in most contexts, a system failure while driving could be catastrophic. Experienced drivers understand that unexpected situations constantly arise on the road. If a dangerous situation emerges that the computer cannot process and the driver must make a quick, intuitive decision, can the driver safely take over control? Moreover, legal problems will inevitably accompany self-driving cars. Questions about liability, applicable laws, and responsibility will divide the public. This uncertainty could provoke extreme reactions from some individuals.

According to various sources, self-driving cars will reach their full potential only when widespread adoption occurs. However, since artificial intelligence taking over humanity is a popular theme in science fiction novels and movies, many people fear that self-driving cars might replicate the scenarios depicted in these stories. The widespread adoption of self-driving cars may take considerable time—perhaps too long. I worry that traffic systems could descend into chaos before adoption reaches the critical mass needed for success.

Additionally, self-driving cars will eliminate many jobs. Taxi drivers, oil companies, and car insurance companies will likely resist this technology rather than embrace it. They will probably join the opposition movement. The crucial question is whether society can maintain stability and harmony until self-driving cars achieve sufficient prevalence to justify the transition.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars represent an exciting technological frontier, we must proceed cautiously. Rather than rushing toward full-scale implementation, we should conduct extensive research, address safety concerns, establish clear legal frameworks, and develop strategies to help displaced workers transition to new opportunities. Only when these prerequisites are met should we consider broader deployment of self-driving cars to the general public.

# The Promise and Peril of Self-Driving Cars

With the ever-increasing number of road accidents, there has been a push towards developing technology that is safer and can make accidents less likely. Since most accidents are caused by distracted driving, technologists are driven to develop self-driving cars. However, it is still not certain whether self-driving cars are really that beneficial to society.

Self-driving cars offer considerable freedom to drivers to perform other tasks while commuting. Drunk-driving will become a thing of the past. Disabled people will not have to depend on others for transportation. Owing to their platooning behavior and complex algorithms that control the distance between vehicles, self-driving cars are believed to reduce the occurrence of road accidents significantly. Although self-driving cars offer many advantages, they also present numerous disadvantages. The new technology will likely increase unemployment rates and damage the gasoline industry. These cars are extremely expensive, and there are high chances of technology failure, meaning safety improvements are not guaranteed.

In my opinion, self-driving cars will not be particularly beneficial for society overall. The primary reason to encourage this technology is that it will ease commuters' lives by reducing commute time through decreased congestion, allowing them to accomplish important tasks while traveling, reducing their dependence on others or public transport, eliminating the need to learn driving skills, and reducing the demand for parking spaces.

However, there are numerous compelling reasons to oppose this technology. Self-driving cars depend entirely on computer technology, sensors, and GPS tracking. When any of these systems fail, the risk of serious accidents increases dramatically. If a vehicle travels at high speed and sensors fail to detect nearby obstacles, accidents are inevitable. GPS technology is often unreliable; for instance, in Pittsburgh, GPS systems frequently detect staircases as roads and recommend them as shortcuts. Without proper computer programming to recognize these pseudo-roads, accidents will occur.

Furthermore, self-driving cars will cause sharp increases in unemployment as taxi drivers, driving instructors, injury lawyers, and insurance company employees lose their jobs. The oil and gas industry will suffer significantly as most of these vehicles will be electric-powered. This economic disruption could have far-reaching consequences for society.

In conclusion, although self-driving cars are promising technological innovations, they are not the complete solution to reducing road accidents and improving commute safety. The risks and societal costs may ultimately outweigh the benefits.

# Self-Driving Cars: Technology, Society, and the Future of Transportation

Debates on self-driving cars have attracted attention from society as a whole. From my perspective, users' adoption and acceptance, safety and privacy concerns, and economic impacts on society are top concerns.

The self-driving car, ideally, can reduce accident rates on the road by overcoming the problem of driver distraction. However, actual performance will largely depend on the underlying technology and algorithms. In addition, external factors including users' attitudes towards automation and weather conditions present threats to the self-driving experience.

On the other hand, the economic impacts of self-driving cars on society present both opportunities and challenges. While improved efficiency and reduced costs could benefit consumers, many jobs in the transportation sector will inevitably be eliminated. Therefore, we must ask: How can we design comprehensive training programs to help vulnerable groups, such as taxi drivers and low-income workers, develop new skills and rebuild their lives in a transformed economy?

Self-driving is not a recent concept. In fact, we are already enjoying the benefits of semi-autonomous vehicles. For example, modern Volvo cars can automatically stop if pedestrians are detected at a risky distance in front of the vehicle when the driver fails to react in time. Currently, there are five recognized levels of driving automation, ranging from no automation to full automation. The highest level represents fully autonomous vehicles, which is the dominant image in public imagination. Whether we can achieve this goal remains unclear. However, investing resources in developing more intelligent and driver-friendly technologies is undoubtedly beneficial.

Beyond developing individual vehicle technologies, constructing a vehicle-to-vehicle communication network system adds significant value to society. Specifically, real-time traffic information and road hazard data can be rapidly distributed across the entire network. In this way, intelligent vehicles can calculate optimal routes and provide personalized navigation based on data collected from passengers, road conditions, and weather patterns. To develop such a communication system, the intelligence level of individual vehicles is essential. Radars, sensors, and software systems must be integrated as a unified team to process and transfer data both within each vehicle and throughout the entire network.

Regarding end-users and consumers, adoption resistance should not pose a major obstacle to widespread implementation. First, as mentioned above, using self-driving cars is not an all-or-nothing choice. Rather, consumers can select which level of automation they wish to use, thereby enhancing their overall driving experience. Second, human-based services are costly, whereas machine-based alternatives offer greater economic efficiency. For instance, people once relied on hiring tax consultants, but now use affordable software instead. Similarly, people can choose between hiring personal drivers, using traditional taxi services, or adopting self-driving alternatives based on their preferences and budgets.

In conclusion, self-driving technologies are already embedded in our daily driving experiences. As development continues, we will increasingly enjoy the safety, efficiency, and convenience benefits that autonomous vehicle technology promises to deliver.

# Self-Driving Cars: Technology's Promise and Pitfalls

As the number of fatalities from distracted and reckless driving increases, the need for safer roadways becomes a primary goal. Self-driving cars, a futuristic technology presently being developed and tested, has many advantages and disadvantages. With each side presently offering valid and convincing points, the debate over the benefits of self-driving cars in society rages on. In a technologically driven world, the incorporation of self-driving vehicles seems likely. The question for the future of self-driving cars will be how many people will accept the new technology as a part of daily life and routine.

Even though the idea of new technology involving self-driving cars can seem exciting and innovative, there are many disadvantages to widely accepting it in society. Since the technology is still being developed and tested, many people raise questions and concerns about the self-driving car's safety and effectiveness. In terms of safety, responsibility becomes questionable should the vehicle get into an accident. The technology itself, with large amounts of stored personal information incorporated within the software, could make people uneasy with worries about identity breaches. In terms of overall effectiveness, the need for new education will become a reality. Current education standards, including driver's education and behind-the-wheel training, could become virtually extinct should self-driving cars become widely adopted. In conjunction with the absence of active driving education, coordinative, peripheral, and basic driving skills will cease to be part of basic knowledge. These disadvantages, along with many others, can create negative associations and opinions of self-driving cars.

With the goal of creating safer and more efficient roadways in mind, self-driving cars offer many advantages to potential consumers. The technology involved is very advanced, with many algorithms controlling the speed, direction, and destination of the vehicle. While controlling these driving factors, this computer also eliminates bad driving behavior usually displayed by humans. These behaviors include, and are not limited to: speeding, drunk driving, driving under the influence of drugs, and distracted driving. In relation to speeding, speed limits could increase with the proliferation of self-driving cars, since the car controls the speed. Since these behaviors are eliminated, car insurance will decrease or cease to exist altogether. When people are not spending time driving, they can spend time doing other activities, such as working, checking their phones, or any other activity that could not be done behind the wheel. These advantages, among others, are revolutionary to the open road as we now know it.

Despite the promising benefits that self-driving cars offer, they are not beneficial to society due to the nature of the technology and its limitations. While there are approximately 33,000 fatalities annually on the roadways, most of these accidents could have been prevented through responsible human behavior. Technology can and has failed, with reliance and dependence on it causing panic when it malfunctions. Unlike technology, people can make conscious choices when it comes to driving. These choices stem from effective education and a functional legal system. Like education, laws would also have to be changed with the emergence of self-driving cars on the road; old rules would give rise to new and more difficult regulations to follow and retain. Self-driving cars are a very new technology, with Google testing the product in a limited number of states. Since Google is the main innovator in this field of technology, other car and gas companies could suffer economically from the rise of electric, self-driving vehicles. Furthermore, the likelihood that these cars will be adopted quickly is unlikely, as people tend to avoid change, especially changes in routine.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars represent an exciting technological advancement with genuine potential to reduce road fatalities and increase efficiency, their widespread adoption faces significant obstacles. The concerns about safety, privacy, liability, and economic disruption cannot be dismissed. Society must carefully balance innovation with caution, ensuring that thorough testing, comprehensive legal frameworks, and public acceptance are in place before self-driving cars become commonplace. Only through such deliberate consideration can we determine whether this technology truly serves the future of transportation and public safety.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Path to Safety and Economic Growth

I take the affirmative position on self-driving cars. I chose this side based on two key arguments: I believe that self-driving cars are safer than human-operated vehicles and that they represent an economically intelligent investment for our society. While I acknowledge the con that self-driving cars can cost more than $100,000 to produce—which exceeds the average American's annual income—I believe this initial expense is outweighed by the long-term safety and economic benefits. Both the pros and cons of this technology ultimately support my argument that self-driving cars will be beneficial to our society.

Safety is the primary advantage of self-driving cars. They will protect not only passengers within the vehicle but also other drivers and their passengers on the road. I believe that driver distractions are the main cause of unsafe driving. Texting and driving is a prime example of how quickly a new hazard can emerge and spread. This dangerous behavior evolved, became prevalent, and was subsequently banned—all within my lifetime. The speed at which texting was outlawed demonstrates how serious its impact was on road safety. However, texting is just one of many distractions. Drivers are still permitted to talk on the phone, adjust the radio, manipulate GPS systems, discipline their children, and converse with passengers. The only way to truly eliminate these risks is to replace the distracted human driver with a reliable computer that cannot be distracted.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars are economically intelligent. The development and production of this technology would create numerous jobs across multiple sectors—engineers, programmers, manufacturers, advertisers, marketers, and salespeople, among others. Historically, every new technology has opened up more jobs than it has eliminated. While critics point out that self-driving cars will be expensive initially, this is a natural part of the technology adoption cycle. When new technologies first emerge, they are expensive and marketed to a limited audience. Over time, production improves and costs decrease, making the technology accessible to the average consumer. The iPhone is an excellent example: it began as an expensive luxury item but is now affordable and ubiquitous.

In conclusion, self-driving cars represent a significant advancement for society. By eliminating driver distractions, they will make our roads safer and reduce accident rates. Simultaneously, they will stimulate economic growth through job creation and technological innovation. The initial high cost is a temporary obstacle that will be overcome as the technology matures. For these reasons, self-driving cars are a beneficial investment in our future.

# Self-Driving Cars: The Future of Safe and Efficient Transportation

As society progresses, more challenges arise that communities demand to be resolved; however, more solutions are now readily available to combat these problems at a highly superior level never seen before. One huge dilemma that society faces today is how to make driving increasingly efficient and roadways much safer than their current state. Recently, technology has advanced enough to bring about a potential solution: the self-driving car. Although there is an abundance of evident positives if the self-driving car were to work properly, many are still concerned that this new piece of technology will not solve today's automobile problems and may bring rise to other issues. Those who support self-driving cars associate most of their claims with the fact that the car will create a safer driving environment, while those against it seek reason to believe that accidents are not completely eliminated. However, the invention of self-driving cars clearly depicts a widened ability and benefit to each and every community that implements this new technology. The utilization of self-driving cars not only provides positives that outweigh potential negatives, but it also allows society to continue to progress and prosper in an extraordinary way.

One huge benefit resulting from the introduction of self-driving cars would be the dramatic improvement of roadway traffic conditions. Through the use of this technology, drivers will feel much safer on the roadways while also enjoying a much more efficient commute. Noticeable improvements that would occur include reduced commute times and limited traffic incidents. According to Eno Transportation, self-driving cars would significantly improve traffic conditions and congestion, helping to reduce commute times while maximizing gasoline usage. By allowing faster speeds on the road, driving time could be reduced considerably. By increasing speed limits on roadways, gasoline is used more efficiently than it is currently, while consequently allowing people to reach their destinations quicker. This contributes to the reduction of traffic seen on major highways—something that is a major factor in automobile accidents. Self-driving cars will also have technology that provides the ability to detect the distance between itself and other vehicles. Sensors in autonomous cars allow vehicles to ride closer together, therefore allowing more cars on the road with actually less traffic. With this capability, cars will be able to ride together at a much closer distance, optimizing time and space to decrease traffic problems significantly.

Although self-driving cars appear to be unmistakably beneficial to society, controversy is still held regarding their implementation. A highly observed criticism of self-driving cars is that car accidents are not completely eliminated on roadways. Critics argue that a self-driving car doesn't completely eliminate the likelihood of a car accident, and many individuals are nervous about handing over all control to a computer, which could malfunction and put drivers in an even more dangerous situation. Many uphold their position that using self-driving cars could pose a significant threat, as car accidents are still likely, yet drivers will feel incompetent to handle these situations. However, what these critics fail to realize is that the technology employed in these vehicles has been tested and refined at the sight of even the most minute malfunctions. In order to be successful, companies will not release a device that is not beyond exceptional to the public. Of course, there will be a percentage of problems that will occur; however, this percentage is so small due to there being no possible way to account for every situation—everything proposes some risk of harm, despite efforts to reduce it.

The technology used within self-driving cars surpasses current technology in the sense that it enables superior behaviors and actions on the road to reduce accidents. Since various algorithms exist within the car's coding, the vehicle gains the ability to process external information like never before to enhance driving safety. Computers use complicated algorithms to determine appropriate stopping distance, distance from other vehicles, and other data that decreases the chances of car accidents dramatically. Through the utilization of such algorithms, the possibility of accidents decreases significantly, as the code is refined to near-flawless precision. As a direct result, traffic problems will not only be reduced but identified if one does occur. In order for the cars to operate most efficiently, they would need to communicate with one another, helping to identify traffic problems or road risks early on. Since nothing can be completely eliminated, algorithms will also account for detection and early recognition to minimize the amount of traffic and problems on the road. Through communication between self-driving cars, an individual vehicle will be able to discover potential threats and change routes early enough—something that current drivers cannot do until it is too late.

With the implementation of this new and innovative technology into society, obvious benefits will arise to help improve current road conditions and reduce risks. Even though there may be some problems that arise from self-driving cars, the positives clearly outweigh the negatives, as driving overall will be safer and more efficient. As a society, members want to ensure that the safety and wellbeing of others not only meet but exceed standards. With the use of self-driving cars, communities will flourish in ways never seen before, creating a safer, more connected, and more efficient transportation system for all.

Self-driving cars have been praised and vilified in the media and society in general, and with good reason. The potential benefits of such a technology are enormous, both when measured in dollars and in human lives. The drawbacks, while mostly speculative, are equally serious. Criticisms come due to a reasonable fear of the unknown. How will self-driving cars operate in unfamiliar conditions? Can we rely on today's GPS systems to provide accurate information to our robotic vehicles? What happens during a malfunction, and will our cars be safe from hackers? All of these questions are reasonable and may even justify a slow-down or regulation of the autonomy vehicle industry. However, I think it's important for us to remember that, no matter what we think, the discussion is not if, but when, robotic cars will enter the mainstream. The future can be delayed, not prevented, and at the end of the day, why would we want to prevent it anyway?

Self-driving cars are just part of a long tradition of disruptive technologies that caused concerns in their times, from the printing press to the industrial revolution. Even within the same industry, there were those who fought the introduction of cars to roadways. It is a little known fact that early road advocates were bicyclists, not drivers. # The Future of Self-Driving Cars: A Society Ready for Change

The discussion of the effects of self-driving cars on society may seem in the distant future, but the future is now. Self-driving cars are gaining popularity with Google and other manufacturing and software companies. As with any new technology, there are certainly reservations on the impact it would have on society as well as the positive effects. The advantages of implementing self-driving cars en masse far outweigh the potential problems associated with them. The time has come to embrace the future with open arms, and it starts with self-driving cars.

Some common concerns with self-driving cars arise from trusting and depending on machines. If humans never have to drive again, then they would forget how to, and this would be deleterious in times of crisis or emergency. Alongside this, people fear a malfunction in software, which may lead to accidents with other cars—human or self-driving. Another gripe with software is the immense capabilities it would have and how much personal information and data it might store. This then leads to distrust in data management and in some cases theft of this data. Humans tend to have an innate distrust of machines, and putting their lives on the line to be driven around is no exception.

Although the aforementioned concerns with self-driving cars are valid, they should not get in the way of the progress of these incredible inventions. There are so many advantages to self-driving cars, but the biggest one may be as simple as time. Self-driving cars would be completely automated and have the potential to link up with other cars on the road and create an almost perfect traffic system where tailgating is safe, high-speed travel is possible, and pedestrian detection is guaranteed. Buying into this system lets society run better, safer, and quicker. In this scenario, commute times would be cut, city driving would be made easier since the car could drop one off and then find its own parking, and users could save countless hours. As if all of these reasons weren't enough, one would have time on the commute to catch up with loved ones, safely eat, read, watch television, and even sleep. Time is precious, and not having to divert precious attention and time to the road allows unprecedented freedom to the user.

While people may still disagree that these benefits are sufficient to change their minds, self-driving cars represent a quicker and safer alternative to public transportation. If society trusts airplanes, trains, subways, and motorized scooters with human lives, then self-driving cars deserve the same consideration. These technologies have demonstrated that automated systems can operate safely and reliably when properly designed and maintained. The transition to self-driving cars would follow a similar pattern of rigorous testing, regulation, and gradual implementation.

Furthermore, the societal benefits extend beyond individual convenience. Reduced traffic congestion would lower fuel consumption and emissions, contributing to environmental sustainability. The elimination of human error—responsible for the vast majority of traffic accidents—could save thousands of lives annually. Additionally, self-driving cars would provide mobility solutions for the elderly, disabled, and those unable to drive, increasing independence and quality of life for vulnerable populations.

In conclusion, while moral dilemmas and technical issues surrounding self-driving cars require continued study and improvement, these challenges should not prevent society from moving forward. The precedent must be established now, as further delay only postpones the benefits that await us. Self-driving cars represent not merely a technological advancement, but a fundamental shift toward a safer, more efficient, and more humane society. The future is here, and it is time we embrace it. Let's give self-driving cars a shot.

The overall advantages of self-driving cars, when implemented without any setbacks, are that they can be safer, cheaper, and more time saving when compared to the standard way of humans manual driving cars. Self-driving cars would be safer because they would eliminate the human element of distracted driving, caused by talking on the cell phone or falling asleep at the wheel. They would also be safer because they would decrease car accidents by being programmed to stop at a safe distance from other vehicles and avoid serious collisions. Self-driving cars would also be cheaper for the government by saving money that would have been spent on transit projects such as building trains. Lastly, self-driving cars would be time saving for both passengers and police officers. Passengers who would now be allowed to rest or perform work on their commute to their destination, and police officers would be able to spend less time catching speeding vehicles on the highway and more time solving more serious crimes.

However, self-driving cars do present significant challenges that must be addressed before widespread implementation. The most pressing disadvantage is the prohibitive cost of creating these vehicles and the complex technology required to ensure safety and prevent malfunctions and hacks. Initial estimates suggest that self-driving cars would cost upwards of $100,000, making them a luxury item accessible only to the upper class and wealthy individuals. If only a smaller percentage of the population can utilize self-driving cars, then the majority of car accidents caused by distracted driving would still occur, and society would fail to realize the promised financial savings. Furthermore, like all technology, self-driving cars carry inherent risks of serious and potentially fatal malfunctions. Although rare, everyday electronics such as iPhones and laptops—technologies that seemed impossible a century ago—do experience problems and failures. Additionally, self-driving cars would face difficulty adapting to new traffic laws or unexpected road work that appears overnight. Perhaps most concerning is the security threat posed by potential hackers who could hijack vehicles and take malicious control of self-driving cars, endangering passengers and pedestrians alike.

Despite these challenges, the advantages of self-driving cars ultimately outweigh their disadvantages. The most compelling factor supporting self-driving car implementation is their potential to dramatically reduce distracted driving, thereby decreasing car accidents and saving lives. A human life is irreplaceable and invaluable—the only thing on earth that cannot be purchased regardless of wealth. Death does not discriminate based on age, gender, religion, race, or social status. output_text:
# Self-Driving Cars: A Dangerous Solution to a Solvable Problem

Not having to worry about paying attention while driving is cool, but dying because your computerized, self-driven car made a mistake is not.

Washington, D.C., Nevada, Florida, Michigan and California are testing cars with artificial intelligence, and they're proving that the hassle creates more danger than the convenience is worth. While it's shown that self-driving cars can prevent accidents caused by human drivers, they don't completely eliminate them and in fact, offer their own set of safety hazards.

According to Auto Insurance Center, "The very security behind self-driving cars would be a major obstacle, especially because the technology would be of very high interest to hackers." This vulnerability exposes drivers and pedestrians alike to potentially catastrophic risks. A hacker could manipulate vehicle systems, causing accidents that would be impossible to predict or prevent.

While car accidents account for a large portion of deaths in the United States, adding the unpredictability of self-driving cars' capabilities for safe driving—as well as the possibility for hackers to cause accidents—only makes matters more hazardous for everyone on the road. The technology simply hasn't matured enough to guarantee safety.

In addition to this new technology being dangerous for everyone involved, implementing it would also be extremely expensive. All current human-driven vehicles would need to be replaced, which requires workers to scrap the old cars and build new ones. Additional training would be necessary for users to understand the self-driving cars and what to do in an emergency. "While the computer takes over once the vehicle is operational, the driver would still be required to maintain some knowledge about how to operate it safely," according to Auto Insurance Center. This paradox undermines the entire purpose of autonomous vehicles.

An important counterpoint to the dangers of autonomous vehicles is that they could significantly improve the lives of people with disabilities by providing easily accessible transportation. Current public transportation methods, while allowing individuals to avoid driving themselves, still exclude those with certain disabilities and are less convenient. For many disabled individuals, self-driving cars represent genuine independence and freedom.

However, the same safety problems still apply to individuals with disabilities, and may even affect them more severely. If an autonomous car were to get in an accident with a disabled passenger, they may not be able to save themselves from the crash or even call for help. Vulnerable populations would bear the greatest burden of this technology's failures.

In conclusion, self-driving cars will be more of a hindrance to society than a help. Not only would they present danger to everyone on the road, but they would be too expensive to implement on a widespread scale. Rather than investing billions into unproven autonomous technology, time and resources would be better spent improving current human-driven cars, enhancing road infrastructure, and creating comprehensive programs to better educate drivers. Until self-driving cars can guarantee complete safety and security, society should continue relying on proven methods of transportation.

# The Risks of Rushing Self-Driving Cars to Market

Behind our evolving society is the driving force of technology, enabling us to do things in our daily lives more quickly and efficiently, saving us time that we consider precious and not to be wasted. When we apply technology to our everyday lives, there are a number of issues that can possibly arise. This can be seen very clearly in the topic of self-driving cars. While there are several eye-catching benefits, such as the possibility of decreased accidents and shortened driving time, there are also a number of drawbacks. These drawbacks include glaringly large expenses, the possibility of technology failure, and concerns regarding the elimination of transportation jobs, like taxi drivers. Unless these problems and questions regarding self-driving cars are resolved, self-driving cars stand as dangerous and overly-ambitious ideas that should not be allowed on the road.

Self-driving cars flaunt how beneficial they can be to society because they can cut down waste and driving time, ultimately saving money in the long-run. Road congestion can be cut down because of the detailed algorithms of these cars that allow them to determine distances from objects and other vehicles. While the computers within the car are so complex that they can do such things on their own, this does not allow them to identify things a human could, like if there were a police officer directing traffic. Another stated benefit of self-driving cars is that they will decrease the number of car accidents, due to human drivers' unstable states of mind or poor choices. Such situations are seen with drivers who are under the influence, or those who are older and have duller senses and reaction times. However, there are still possibilities of accidents occurring with these cars. The likelihood of a car accident is not zero, despite how hi-tech and accurate these cars are. For one, drivers who are in an unstable state of mind may not be able to take control of their car in the case that the self-driving technology malfunctions. In addition, under certain weather conditions, the car's level of safety is compromised. For example, the sensor atop the car's roof can be damaged by heavy rain. This leads to a bigger question: what will happen once the self-driving technology fails? The role that the human driver plays when paired with a self-driving car is ambiguous. The self-driving car is indeed a piece of autonomous technology that strips some control from its human drivers.

The financial burden of self-driving cars presents another significant obstacle to their widespread adoption. These vehicles are extraordinarily expensive to develop, manufacture, and maintain, making them inaccessible to the average consumer. The cost of the sophisticated sensors, processors, and software required to operate these vehicles safely adds tens of thousands of dollars to the price of a single car. Furthermore, the infrastructure needed to support self-driving cars—including updated road systems and communication networks—would require massive government investment. For many families struggling with transportation costs, self-driving cars would remain a luxury they cannot afford, potentially widening the gap between wealthy and working-class citizens.

Beyond financial concerns, the social and economic consequences of widespread self-driving car adoption cannot be ignored. Millions of people worldwide depend on driving-related jobs for their livelihoods, including taxi drivers, truck drivers, and delivery personnel. The introduction of self-driving cars would eliminate these positions, leaving workers without income or job security. While some argue that new jobs would be created in technology and maintenance sectors, there is no guarantee that displaced workers would have access to retraining programs or that new positions would be available in their communities. The transition could lead to widespread unemployment and economic hardship for entire families and regions.

Often people are so easily infatuated with the bright, shiny pieces of technology that emerge onto the mainstream, whether it be the newest smartphone or pair of headphones. With self-driving cars, it is important not to be swept up in the wild dreams and goals for these machines. While their potential is certainly something to explore and inspect, the implementation of these cars is too premature and would not be beneficial to society, as there are too many unresolved issues requiring much time and effort to thoroughly address. Before self-driving cars are permitted on public roads, manufacturers must demonstrate that the technology is completely reliable, that adequate safeguards exist for human intervention, and that society has a plan to support workers whose jobs will be displaced. Until these critical challenges are met, the risks of self-driving cars far outweigh their benefits, and caution must prevail over innovation.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Promising Solution for Modern Transportation

It is 3 a.m. in the morning after drinking with your best friends at junior high. You are exhausted, lost, and intoxicated in the middle of nowhere. You want to drive back home, but that is simply too dangerous. A self-driving car could take you home safely. This is a rather new area and quite debatable. How can we ensure that robots will navigate all the complicated situations on the roads? What if there are other humans involved, such as other drivers or police giving signals? Most importantly, how can we use them effectively? Indeed, there are many uncertainties related to self-driving cars, but overall it is a promising technology. Self-driving cars can eliminate distractions, help elderly and disabled people maintain mobility, and save energy and time.

First of all, let us address doubts about self-driving algorithms. Even though the situation on the road is unpredictable, many scenarios can be highly generalized. For example, if a sensor detects an obstacle ahead, regardless of whether it is a cat or a pedestrian, the vehicle will stop immediately. The same principle applies to interactions with other human drivers. Moreover, since learning to drive is already difficult for many people, programming a robot to drive should not be significantly more complex.

Human drivers are easily distracted. As researchers have noted, "Many traffic accidents are preventable, and an alarming number of them result from distracted driving." In contrast, it is much harder for a computer to become distracted by a beautiful view along the road. In this regard, AI is far more reliable than humans. Human drivers may be impaired by alcohol or drugs, affected by their emotional state—from depression to euphoria—or preoccupied with conversations with passengers. If AI takes control of driving, humans can spend less time on the road and more time focusing on their careers and personal pursuits, which benefits everyone involved.

Additionally, many people rely heavily on public transportation or family members to travel because they cannot drive independently due to physical limitations. This challenge becomes increasingly urgent as our population ages. In my own childhood, I was often forced to drive my grandmother to her home, which consumed hours I could have spent elsewhere. Moreover, my grandmother could only travel once a week because my father was too busy to drive her more frequently. Imagine the freedom and independence that self-driving cars would provide to elderly and disabled individuals.

Furthermore, AI can calculate optimal routes efficiently. In computer science, we learn about algorithms such as minimal spanning trees to find the shortest path. However, few drivers apply graph theory before driving. Self-driving cars would ensure that passengers always take the most efficient route to school or work. This would reduce time spent on the road and decrease fuel consumption, thereby protecting our environment.

In conclusion, self-driving cars represent a transformative technology that addresses multiple societal challenges. By eliminating human error, enabling mobility for those who cannot drive, and optimizing routes for efficiency, autonomous vehicles promise a safer, more equitable, and more sustainable future for transportation. While uncertainties remain, the potential benefits far outweigh the concerns, making self-driving cars a worthy investment for society.

# The Promise of Self-Driving Cars in Reducing Road Accidents

With the increasing number of cars on the road and people becoming more careless about safety, the number of fatalities due to accidents is also increasing. Even experienced drivers fall into a false sense of confidence and drive casually without proper attention. Moreover, increasing stress levels and substance abuse among the young generation are common nowadays. All these factors result in distractions while driving, thereby increasing the number of road accidents.

Self-driving cars can revolutionize transportation by ensuring safe and fully focused driving every time. A computer operates consistently behind the wheel, unaffected by external distractions and overconfidence. Each time it drives, it takes the same precautions and maintains the same level of safety as the first time. Moreover, artificial intelligence learns from others' mistakes and retains data to make better decisions in the future.

The benefits of autonomous vehicles extend beyond safety. While commuting to work, most people are mentally preoccupied with work deadlines or concerns about their jobs, making traffic violations and distractions common. With computers controlling vehicles, this problem will be eliminated, allowing people to focus on important tasks during their commute. Additionally, disabled individuals who currently rely on others or public transportation will gain greater independence and mobility.

Self-driving cars will also significantly impact law enforcement and traffic management. Police officers currently spend considerable time tracking vehicles, issuing traffic violations, and managing congestion. With autonomous vehicles following traffic rules strictly and all vehicles connected through computer systems, traffic management becomes simpler, allowing police to focus on more serious crimes.

Furthermore, self-driving cars can reduce fuel consumption and energy wastage. While humans often leave engines running at traffic lights and stops, computers can optimize fuel efficiency automatically.

In conclusion, self-driving cars offer transformative benefits: they save lives, conserve energy, save time, and allow people to focus on meaningful activities rather than driving. By reducing road accidents, alleviating traffic congestion, and providing safe transportation for both passengers and pedestrians, autonomous vehicles represent a promising solution to modern transportation challenges.

draft1:
Today's world is punctuated by new innovations and technologies being released every day. An intriguing one that is generating equal interest and consternation across the United States is the technology of self-driving cars. Supporters of self-driving cars note that it would improve fuel efficiency and drive down gasoline costs to consumers, that it would dramatically reduce car-related morbidity and mortality, and that it would allow disabled and elderly individuals independence and the ability to travel freely unlike they could before. However on the opposite side of things, self-driving cars pose new risks and concerns as well. Concerns about them include their inability to operate in adverse weather conditions, their inability to handle unique road conditions and human traffic signals when mechanical traffic signals aren't present, the high cost associated with the vehicles, and the general wariness that many consumers have for self-driving cars and if the technology would ever even be widely accepted enough for use.

Personally, I find the benefits to self-driving cars vastly outweigh the negative factors. Self-driving cars provide a safe, efficient platform that can reduce motor vehicle accident associated fatalities and injuries significantly, reduce the time of commutes and the cost associated with extended periods of driving, and allows people who aren't able to drive such as the disabled and elderly a newfound freedom that so many who have access to cars take for granted. The cost of human life that can be saved is alone the best supporting reason for self driving cars. It has been reported that up to 81% of motor vehicle accidents are due to human error, and this could be completely mitigated by computers who don't have the capability to be distracted, tired, drunk, or any other factor that reduces a human's ability to drive safely. By taking the human factor out of driving, countless lives and debilitating injuries can be prevented with self-driving cars. Additionally, self-driving cars have the potential to revolutionize traffic patterns via their ability to "platoon" where they drive in coordinated packs together. This coordination allows more cars to be on the road as they can drive closer together in a safer manner than humans ever could, It also removes the human inefficiency of driving that causes traffic jams and congestion and would therefore reduce commute times and optimize fuel efficiency to save consumers more money on gasoline. And lastly comes the ability of self-cars to substantially improve the lives of society's least mobile individuals such as the elderly and disabled by granting them newfound independence. Many of us who can walk, drive, and see, etc normally take for granted our ability to move and don't realize how dramatically self-driving cars can improve their lives. No longer would they have to wait for public access transit vans that are known for being slow and extremely limited in hours and range.

Of course however, there are negative factors to self-driving cars that are valid and warrant important consideration as well. Opponents to self-driving cars note that the technology just isn't ready yet, self-driving cars still can't handle poor weather conditions such as heavy rain, they cannot accommodate for human traffic signals when regular traffic signals fail, and that there is still a general opposition to the idea of drivers relinquishing control of their cars to a computer which could potentially put them in dangerous situations. However, many of these concerns can be remedied with time and further development. As the technology matures and barriers are hurdled, these risks will be mitigated and self-driving cars can progressively become widely accepted.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Solution Too Soon for America

Far too often, the media presents heart-wrenching stories of innocent people killed in car accidents from drunk drivers or distracted drivers. Who would argue against a solution to these preventable problems derived from careless human mistakes? One proposed yet controversial solution is implementing self-driving cars. While many benefits exist, I still do not support their use in society. The benefits of self-driving cars are easily understood: they greatly reduce human error, which is the result of 81% of car accidents. Minor accidents like rear-endings would decrease because the computers of the cars would use algorithms to calculate appropriate stopping distances. As The New York Times suggests, self-driving cars would greatly help elderly and disabled people travel. The stress of parking would decrease as self-driving cars dropped people off near their destination. Self-driving cars would also reduce traffic congestion by electronically communicating in a process called "platooning" and enable more people to be on the road at once. Speed limits could even increase as computers in the car can manage safe driving at higher speeds than humans.

However, many significant drawbacks accompany these potential benefits. First, self-driving cars currently carry a prohibitive price tag exceeding $100,000, limiting access to wealthy Americans and making widespread adoption unlikely in the near future. Furthermore, without near-universal adoption, self-driving cars cannot achieve their maximum potential. If only a small percentage of vehicles are autonomous, they cannot effectively platoon with human-operated cars, eliminating one of their greatest advantages for reducing traffic and fuel consumption.

Beyond cost and infrastructure concerns, practical limitations threaten the safety that self-driving advocates promise. Inclement weather significantly impairs sensor abilities, putting passengers and other road users at risk during storms. GPS systems and traffic signals can malfunction, leaving self-driving cars vulnerable to technological failures beyond their control. Additionally, both passengers and other drivers report increased anxiety about potential malfunctions, which could undermine public trust in the technology.

The ethical and economic implications of self-driving cars present perhaps the most troubling obstacles to their implementation. When accidents occur between autonomous and human-operated vehicles, determining liability becomes complicated and ambiguous. Should blame fall on the vehicle's programming, the manufacturer, or the passenger? These unanswered questions could lead to costly litigation and public backlash. Moreover, widespread adoption would devastate the job market for millions of professional drivers, mechanics, and workers in the gasoline industry, creating economic hardship without adequate transition plans.

The fundamental problem is that self-driving cars cannot deliver their promised benefits during a transition period. America needs either complete or near-complete adoption to truly reap the rewards of reduced accidents, decreased congestion, and lower emissions. However, the high cost, public hesitation, and technological limitations make rapid adoption unlikely. Instead, we would face years of tension and danger as autonomous vehicles share the road with human drivers, with few of the benefits and many of the risks.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars represent an intriguing technological advancement, American society is not yet ready for their implementation. The transition phase would be prolonged and problematic, delivering minimal benefits while maintaining significant risks. Rather than rushing to adopt this technology, we should focus on improving driver education, strengthening traffic enforcement, and developing other safety measures that can be implemented immediately. Only when production costs decrease substantially and the technology proves reliable in all conditions should America reconsider embracing self-driving cars.

Trey Anastasio once said "things don't go on forever, and the quicker you accept that change is inevitable, the happier you're going to be". Since the dawn of the industrial revolution, life has changed dramatically: horse and buggies to cars, radio to television, telegraph to computers that fit in your pocket. As technology has developed over time, we have integrated it more and more in our daily lives, and that technology has in-turn brought ease, entertainment, and safety. Handing over the semblance of control one has while driving to a car which would drive itself can bring about trepidation, but when accounting for the increase in both safety and time, it can begin to look like the exceptional revolution that it is.   

Humans are prone to error in everything that they do--and driving is no exception. Eighty-one percent of motor vehicle accidents today are a result of human error. Whether they are distracted due to their environment (other passengers in the car, looking for something, a large number of cars on the road), their emotions (road rage, anxiety), or their physiology (tired, under the influence of alcohol/drugs), they are not putting the amount of attention into driving that a self driving car could do. Allowing the well tested software to do the driving increases the safety of the roads. If there are safer roads, police would be able to shift their attention from speeding tickets to larger issues, like crime and neighborhood safety. Whether on the road or off, self driving cars allow for an increase in safety. 

According to Newsroom, the average American spends 17,600 minutes a year behind the wheel. When converted into hours, then days that number equates to a little over 12 days each year. Now imagine being able to reclaim that time, being able to take the attention that you used to to operate your vehicle and give it to something more meaningful. This could be reading the newspaper or a new book, talking to friends or family, eating a meal that maybe you missed, anything you could do sitting on your couch at home.  As the quantity of self driving cars on the road increases, so does the safety of highways and roads that typically become congested at peak driving times. This increase in safety could eventually lead to increased speed limits, decreasing the time spent commuting to and from work. Self driving cars give you your time back.

There are arguments against the implementation of self driving cars which are concerned with the reduction of business for the gas industry and personal injury lawyers. And yes those are people's jobs, but the success of those vocations is contingent upon destruction--whether that be a person becoming injured or the planet succumbing to global warming and pollution. While self driving cars could increase the safety and fuel efficiency of cars, working to reduce destruction.  

Self driving cars are a large change for the average person, but change is not always unfavorable. The widespread implementation of self driving cars would bring safety on and off the road, as well as decrease the amount of and increase the quality of time you spend in your vehicle.

# Self-Driving Cars: The Inevitable Revolution

Trey Anastasio once said "things don't go on forever, and the quicker you accept that change is inevitable, the happier you're going to be". Since the dawn of the industrial revolution, life has changed dramatically: horse and buggies to cars, radio to television, telegraph to computers that fit in your pocket. As technology has developed over time, we have integrated it more and more in our daily lives, and that technology has in-turn brought ease, entertainment, and safety. Handing over the semblance of control one has while driving to a car which would drive itself can bring about trepidation, but when accounting for the increase in both safety and time, it can begin to look like the exceptional revolution that it is.

Humans are prone to error in everything that they do--and driving is no exception. Eighty-one percent of motor vehicle accidents today are a result of human error. Whether they are distracted due to their environment (other passengers in the car, looking for something, a large number of cars on the road), their emotions (road rage, anxiety), or their physiology (tired, under the influence of alcohol/drugs), they are not putting the amount of attention into driving that a self driving car could do. Allowing the well tested software to do the driving increases the safety of the roads. If there are safer roads, police would be able to shift their attention from speeding tickets to larger issues, like crime and neighborhood safety. Whether on the road or off, self driving cars allow for an increase in safety.

According to Newsroom, the average American spends 17,600 minutes a year behind the wheel. When converted into hours, then days that number equates to a little over 12 days each year. Now imagine being able to reclaim that time, being able to take the attention that you used to dedicate to operating your vehicle and give it to something more meaningful. This could be reading the newspaper or a new book, talking to friends or family, eating a meal that maybe you missed, anything you could do sitting on your couch at home. As the quantity of self driving cars on the road increases, so does the safety of highways and roads that typically become congested at peak driving times. This increase in safety could eventually lead to increased speed limits, decreasing the time spent commuting to and from work. Self driving cars give you your time back.

There are arguments against the implementation of self driving cars which are concerned with the reduction of business for the gas industry and personal injury lawyers. And yes, those are people's jobs, but the success of those vocations is contingent upon destruction--whether that be a person becoming injured or the planet succumbing to global warming and pollution. While self driving cars could increase the safety and fuel efficiency of vehicles, they work to reduce destruction rather than perpetuate it.

Self driving cars represent a large change for the average person, but change is not always unfavorable. The widespread implementation of self driving cars would bring increased safety on and off the road, as well as decrease the amount of time spent driving while increasing the quality of that time. In embracing this technological shift, we honor Anastasio's wisdom: we accept the inevitable and position ourselves to be happier for it.

As with many technologies associated with automation, there are both costs and advantages to self-driving cars. In theory they would create a safer, more cost-effective driving experience and would eliminate many of the tragic accidents that contribute to one of the leading causes of death in modern society. While these benefits come at many costs, some of which we cannot even predict, I believe self-driving cars would be overall beneficial to society. 

It would be naive to think that self-driving cars wouldn't cause any car accidents. There would still be risks of malfunction due to glitches, bad weather, or not being able to take into account human traffic signals, but these risks would come nowhere near the risks associated with humans driving cars. In fact, according to the article, 81 percent of car accidents are attributable to human error. Taking human error out of the equation would dramatically reduce the amount of deaths and injuries that result from car crashes and that shatter families, schools and entire communities. 

The cost-effectiveness of self-driving cars goes beyond just saving money from insurance and healthcare costs. By reducing traffic, money could be saved on gas. By reducing the need for police officers to be patrolling the roads, these resources could be allocated elsewhere. And, finally, without having to focus the majority of their attention on driving, drivers would be able to use that time to do other important things.

Self-driving cars would make transportation more accessible for everyone. Disabled individuals, the elderly, as well as people in communities with little access to other forms of public transportation would benefit. Further, self-driving cars would act as designated drivers for people who are intoxicated, eliminating the issue of drunk driving. 

Though self-driving cars could put people who work in the transportation sector out of work, this would be a gradual process that would go hand in hand with new employment or training opportunities for those employees, many of whom only work in this sector part-time. Further, this kind of argument is also used in matters of climate change by people who believe that the jobs of those currently in the coal and natural gas industries are more important than the dire need to switch to renewable energy sources in order to curb climate change. We have to ask ourselves whether the employment of people in the transportation sector and the fallout they would experience in a world with self-driving cars is more important than the potential to save hundreds of thousands of lives a year.

# Self-Driving Cars: A Beneficial Future for Society

As with many technologies associated with automation, there are both costs and advantages to self-driving cars. In theory they would create a safer, more cost-effective driving experience and would eliminate many of the tragic accidents that contribute to one of the leading causes of death in modern society. While these benefits come at many costs, some of which we cannot even predict, I believe self-driving cars would be overall beneficial to society. 

It would be naive to think that self-driving cars wouldn't cause any car accidents. There would still be risks of malfunction due to glitches, bad weather, or not being able to take into account human traffic signals, but these risks would come nowhere near the risks associated with humans driving cars. In fact, according to the article, 81 percent of car accidents are attributable to human error. Taking human error out of the equation would dramatically reduce the amount of deaths and injuries that result from car crashes and that shatter families, schools and entire communities. 

The cost-effectiveness of self-driving cars goes beyond just saving money from insurance and healthcare costs. By reducing traffic, money could be saved on gas. By reducing the need for police officers to be patrolling the roads, these resources could be allocated elsewhere. And, finally, without having to focus the majority of their attention on driving, drivers would be able to use that time to do other important things.

Self-driving cars would make transportation more accessible for everyone. Disabled individuals, the elderly, as well as people in communities with little access to other forms of public transportation would benefit. Further, self-driving cars would act as designated drivers for people who are intoxicated, eliminating the issue of drunk driving. 

Though self-driving cars could put people who work in the transportation sector out of work, this would be a gradual process that would go hand in hand with new employment or training opportunities for those employees, many of whom only work in this sector part-time. Further, this kind of argument is also used in matters of climate change by people who believe that the jobs of those currently in the coal and natural gas industries are more important than the dire need to switch to renewable energy sources in order to curb climate change. We have to ask ourselves whether the employment of people in the transportation sector and the fallout they would experience in a world with self-driving cars is more important than the potential to save hundreds of thousands of lives a year.

In conclusion, while the transition to self-driving cars will undoubtedly present challenges, the evidence overwhelmingly supports their adoption. The dramatic reduction in accidents and fatalities, combined with significant economic benefits and improved accessibility, far outweighs the concerns about job displacement. Society has consistently chosen progress and safety over maintaining outdated systems, and self-driving cars represent the next necessary step in that evolution. By embracing this technology, we have the opportunity to save countless lives and create a more equitable and efficient transportation system for all.
